blurb: Builds and maintains the Government of Alberta's relationships with First Nations, Métis Settlements, and Métis communities — managing Crown consultation on land and resource decisions, supporting Indigenous economic and community development, administering Métis Settlement governance, and leading reconciliation initiatives.

goals.title: OUTCOME 1: Indigenous communities are meaningfully consulted on land and resource decisions
goals.description: The ministry manages Alberta's legal duty to consult with First Nations and Métis communities when provincial decisions on Crown land and natural resource management may affect Treaty rights, traditional uses, or harvesting activities.

foundational_services.name: Administer Crown consultation policy and the consultation assessment process
foundational_services.desc: Operates the Aboriginal Consultation Office, which manages consultation assessments for resource development, infrastructure, and land management decisions across the province. Maintains consultation policies for First Nations (Treaties 6, 7, 8), Métis Settlements, and Credibly Asserted Métis Communities.
foundational_services.users: Government regulators, industry proponents, Indigenous communities

main_services.name: Participate in consultation on a land or resource decision

supporting.name: Respond to a consultation notification on a proposed project or land decision
supporting.desc: When a land or resource decision may affect Treaty rights or traditional uses, First Nations and Métis communities receive notification and can participate in consultation. Communities can raise concerns, request information, and propose accommodation measures.
supporting.users: First Nations and Métis community leadership, consultation coordinators

reach_estimate.basis: All Crown land and resource decisions potentially affecting 48 First Nations and 8 Métis Settlements

economic_impact.note: Meaningful consultation upholds Treaty rights and provides regulatory certainty for resource development

supporting.name: Submit a consultation assessment request as a project proponent
supporting.desc: Industry proponents and government agencies submit consultation assessment requests through the ACO Digital Service when a proposed project or decision triggers the duty to consult. The ACO determines the scope and level of consultation required.
supporting.users: Energy, mining, forestry, and infrastructure proponents

reach_estimate.basis: All major resource and infrastructure projects in Alberta require consultation assessment

economic_impact.note: Consultation assessment is a prerequisite for regulatory approval of resource projects

relationships.service: Respond to a consultation notification on a proposed project or land decision

supporting.name: Access consultation capacity funding as an Indigenous community
supporting.desc: The Indigenous Consultation Capacity Program provides annual funding to First Nations and Métis communities to support their ability to participate effectively in Alberta's consultation process, including hiring staff, reviewing technical documents, and engaging in field studies.
supporting.users: First Nations and Métis communities participating in consultation

reach_estimate.estimate: Funding distributed to dozens of communities annually
reach_estimate.basis: Program supports all Indigenous communities engaged in consultation

economic_impact.note: Capacity funding ensures communities can participate meaningfully in technical consultation processes

goals.title: OUTCOME 2: Indigenous communities have economic opportunities and strong community supports
goals.description: The ministry supports Indigenous economic development through business investment, employment partnerships, development grants, reconciliation initiatives, and community safety programs — helping First Nations and Métis communities build prosperity and resilience.

foundational_services.name: Develop Indigenous economic development and reconciliation policy
foundational_services.desc: Develops policy frameworks for Indigenous economic partnerships, coordinates the government's response to the Truth and Reconciliation Commission's Calls to Action and the National Inquiry into Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls, and supports cross-ministry Indigenous initiatives.
foundational_services.users: Government policy staff, cross-ministry working groups

main_services.name: Access funding for Indigenous economic and community development

supporting.name: Apply for a First Nations community development grant
supporting.desc: First Nations can apply for grants through the First Nations Development Fund for economic, social, and community development projects. The fund, sourced from First Nations gaming revenues, supports infrastructure, education, health, cultural preservation, and economic diversification.
supporting.users: First Nations governments and community organizations

reach_estimate.estimate: Dozens of grants awarded annually across 48 First Nations
reach_estimate.basis: Major funding source for First Nations community development projects

economic_impact.note: Gaming-revenue grants fund critical community infrastructure and economic diversification

supporting.name: Apply for Indigenous business investment funding
supporting.desc: Indigenous community-owned businesses can apply for investment funding through the Aboriginal Business Investment Fund, which provides up to $10 million per year for business ventures that create employment and economic opportunities in Indigenous communities.
supporting.users: Indigenous community-owned businesses and economic development corporations

reach_estimate.basis: $10 million annual fund for Indigenous community business ventures

economic_impact.note: Business investment creates jobs and builds self-sustaining Indigenous economies

supporting.name: Access employment and training supports for Indigenous workers
supporting.desc: The Employment Partnership Program supports Indigenous people in accessing training, skills development, and employment opportunities. Funding supports community-based employment projects, skills training, and partnerships between Indigenous organizations and employers.
supporting.users: Indigenous job seekers, training providers, employers, community organizations

reach_estimate.basis: Ongoing employment partnership projects across the province

economic_impact.note: Closing the Indigenous employment gap strengthens communities and the provincial economy

main_services.name: Support Indigenous community safety and reconciliation

supporting.name: Apply for community safety funding addressing violence against Indigenous women and girls
supporting.desc: Indigenous-led organizations can apply for funding through the Community Support Fund for projects that address violence and promote the safety and economic security of Indigenous women, girls, and two-spirit people, responding to the Calls for Justice from the National Inquiry into MMIWG.

reach_estimate.basis: Dedicated fund addressing Calls for Justice

economic_impact.note: Targeted funding addresses systemic safety gaps for Indigenous women, girls, and 2S+ people

supporting.name: Access reconciliation and cultural preservation project funding
supporting.desc: The Indigenous Reconciliation Initiative supports Indigenous-led cultural revitalization, language preservation, and reconciliation projects. Funding helps communities maintain and strengthen cultural practices, oral histories, and intergenerational knowledge transfer.
supporting.users: Indigenous cultural organizations, community groups, elders' councils

reach_estimate.estimate: Dozens of cultural and reconciliation projects funded annually
reach_estimate.basis: Growing investment in reconciliation and cultural preservation

economic_impact.note: Cultural preservation strengthens community identity and supports reconciliation goals

goals.title: OUTCOME 3: Métis Settlements are self-governing, sustainable communities
goals.description: The ministry administers the Métis Settlements Act, supporting the governance, land management, and membership processes of Alberta's eight Métis Settlements — the only legislated Métis land base in Canada, covering 1.25 million acres.

foundational_services.name: Administer the Métis Settlements Act and support Settlement governance
foundational_services.desc: Administers the legislative and regulatory framework for the eight Métis Settlements, supports the Métis Settlements General Council, coordinates the Joint Review Committee, and manages the provincial relationship with Settlement councils on governance, land, and financial administration.
foundational_services.users: Métis Settlement councils, Métis Settlements General Council

main_services.name: Access Métis Settlement membership and land services

supporting.name: Apply for membership in a Métis Settlement
supporting.desc: Individuals who meet the eligibility criteria under the Métis Settlements Act can apply for membership in one of Alberta's eight Métis Settlements. Membership provides the right to reside on Settlement land, participate in governance, and access Settlement programs and services.
supporting.users: Métis individuals eligible for Settlement membership

reach_estimate.estimate: Hundreds of membership applications across 8 settlements
reach_estimate.basis: Nearly 6,000 current Settlement members with ongoing new applications

economic_impact.note: Membership is the foundation of Settlement self-governance and access to the Métis land base

supporting.name: Obtain a land allocation or resolve a land matter on a Métis Settlement
supporting.desc: Settlement members can apply for land allocations for residential or other purposes under the Métis Settlements Act. Land disputes and matters are handled through Settlement councils and, if needed, the Métis Settlements Appeal Tribunal.
supporting.users: Métis Settlement members seeking land allocations or resolving disputes

channels.label: Land allocation application through Settlement council

reach_estimate.estimate: Dozens of land matters annually across 8 settlements
reach_estimate.basis: 1.25 million acres of Settlement land with ongoing allocation and management

economic_impact.note: Secure land tenure is fundamental to Settlement sustainability and self-governance

supporting.name: Appeal a membership or land decision to the Métis Settlements Appeal Tribunal
supporting.desc: Settlement members or applicants who disagree with a membership, land allocation, or other council decision can appeal to the independent Métis Settlements Appeal Tribunal established under the Métis Settlements Act.
supporting.users: Settlement members or applicants challenging a council decision

reach_estimate.basis: Independent tribunal hearing appeals from 8 Settlement councils

economic_impact.note: Independent appeal process ensures fairness and accountability in Settlement governance

supporting.name: Access information about Métis Settlement governance and your rights
supporting.desc: Settlement members and the public can access information about how Métis Settlements are governed, the role of Settlement councils and the General Council, land protection, financial administration, and the rights and obligations of Settlement members under the Métis Settlements Act.

reach_estimate.estimate: Thousands of page views and information requests annually
reach_estimate.basis: Public interest in the unique Métis Settlement governance model

economic_impact.note: Informed members participate more effectively in Settlement self-governance
